subject: EU Law
module_title: Sources and Hierarchy of EU Law
lesson_title: Primary Law: The Treaties and the Charter

questions.question: Primary law of the EU consists of:

options.A: Regulations and Directives adopted under the ordinary legislative procedure
options.B: The founding Treaties (TEU and TFEU), Protocols and Annexes attached to the Treaties, and the Charter of Fundamental Rights
options.C: Decisions of the Court of Justice that have been formally recognised by the European Council
options.D: General principles of law as developed by the Court of Justice

questions.explanation: Primary EU law — the highest tier of the EU legal hierarchy — comprises the TEU, TFEU (and their predecessors), Protocols and Annexes attached to the Treaties (which have the same legal value), accession treaties, and the Charter of Fundamental Rights (given Treaty value by Article 6(1) TEU). It cannot be superseded by secondary legislation.

questions.question: The Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU became legally binding when:

options.A: It was proclaimed in Nice in 2000
options.B: The Lisbon Treaty entered into force in December 2009, which gave it the same legal value as the Treaties under Article 6(1) TEU
options.C: The Constitutional Treaty was ratified in 2004
options.D: The Court of Justice held in a landmark 2005 ruling that it had binding force

questions.explanation: The Charter was originally proclaimed at Nice in 2000 as a political declaration without binding legal force. It became legally binding when the Lisbon Treaty entered into force on 1 December 2009, which by Article 6(1) TEU conferred on it the same legal value as the Treaties.

questions.question: Article 6(3) TEU provides that fundamental rights, as guaranteed by the ECHR and as they result from constitutional traditions common to member states, shall constitute general principles of the Union's law. This means:

options.A: The ECHR is directly incorporated into EU law as primary law
options.B: The EU has formally acceded to the ECHR
options.C: These rights inform the interpretation of EU law as general principles, even though the ECHR itself is not part of EU law
options.D: National constitutional rights automatically override conflicting EU law

questions.explanation: Article 6(3) TEU establishes the ECHR and national constitutional traditions as sources of general principles of EU law. This does not make the ECHR directly part of EU law — the EU has not (yet) acceded to the ECHR despite Article 6(2) TEU providing for accession. Opinion 2/13 (2014) found that the draft accession agreement was incompatible with the EU Treaties.

questions.question: Protocols annexed to the Treaties have what legal status?

options.A: They are secondary legislation with lower status than the Treaties
options.B: They have the same legal value as the Treaties to which they are annexed
options.D: They can be amended by the Council acting by unanimity, without a full IGC

questions.explanation: Article 51 TEU: Protocols and Annexes to the Treaties form an integral part of the Treaties. They therefore have the same legal value as the Treaty articles themselves. Examples include Protocol 30 (on Poland and the UK regarding the Charter), Protocol on the application of the principles of subsidiarity and proportionality, and Protocol on the Statute of the Court of Justice.

questions.question: The Charter of Fundamental Rights applies to member states:

options.A: In all circumstances, whenever domestic law is in question
options.B: Only when member states are implementing EU law
options.C: Only when member states are derogating from EU fundamental freedoms
options.D: Only when member states are parties to proceedings before the CJEU

questions.explanation: Article 51(1) of the Charter: the Charter applies to 'the institutions, bodies, offices and agencies of the Union' and to 'the Member States only when they are implementing Union law'. The Court confirmed in Åkerberg Fransson (C-617/10) that 'implementing EU law' covers all situations where member states act within the scope of EU law.

questions.question: The primary law principle of sincere cooperation (Article 4(3) TEU) requires member states to:

options.A: Harmonise their national laws with each other without EU involvement
options.B: Take all appropriate measures to fulfil their Treaty obligations and to facilitate achievement of the Union's tasks, and refrain from measures that would jeopardise Union objectives
options.C: Submit all proposed national legislation to the Commission for approval before enacting it
options.D: Implement EU Directives by a method of their choice within the timescale set by national parliaments

questions.explanation: Article 4(3) TEU (the principle of sincere cooperation or loyal cooperation) has three limbs: member states shall take all appropriate measures to ensure fulfilment of Treaty obligations; they shall facilitate achievement of EU tasks; and they shall refrain from any measure which could jeopardise the attainment of the Union's objectives. It has been used by the Court to develop State liability (Francovich) and the duty of consistent interpretation.

questions.question: Ordinary treaty revision under Article 48 TEU requires:

options.A: A qualified majority in the Council and simple majority in the Parliament
options.B: An Intergovernmental Conference, followed by ratification by all member states in accordance with their constitutional requirements
options.C: Unanimous agreement of the Council, approved by a two-thirds majority of the Parliament
options.D: A referendum in each member state, with a majority in favour

questions.explanation: Article 48 TEU (ordinary revision procedure): amendments are proposed by any member state, the Parliament, or the Commission; the European Council convenes an IGC; amendments enter into force after ratification by all member states in accordance with their respective constitutional requirements (which may involve parliamentary votes, referendums, or both depending on national constitutional requirements).

questions.question: The hierarchy of norms in EU law means:

options.A: General principles always take precedence over Treaty provisions
options.B: Secondary legislation must not conflict with primary law (the Treaties, Charter, and general principles); if it does, it may be annulled by the Court of Justice
options.C: Regulations always override Directives, regardless of content or legal base
options.D: Member state constitutional provisions override inconsistent EU secondary legislation

questions.explanation: The EU legal hierarchy (primary law → secondary law) means that Regulations, Directives, Decisions, and other secondary acts must comply with the Treaties and Charter. The Court of Justice reviews secondary legislation for compatibility under Article 263 TFEU. Conflicting secondary legislation is subject to annulment — it does not automatically become primary law.

questions.question: The 'passerelle' clauses in the Treaties allow:

options.A: The Commission to adopt legislative acts without Council approval in urgent situations
options.B: The European Council (or Council) to move from unanimity to QMV, or from a special to the ordinary legislative procedure, in specified areas, without a full treaty amendment
options.C: Member states to opt out of secondary legislation they consider contrary to national interests
options.D: The Parliament to reject Commission proposals without a formal legislative procedure

questions.explanation: Passerelle clauses (from the French for 'footbridge') allow the European Council or Council to switch from unanimity to QMV or from special to ordinary legislative procedure in certain areas (e.g., multi-annual financial framework, CFSP). This avoids the full Article 48 revision procedure but requires unanimity in the European Council and no national parliament objection.

questions.question: The Court of Justice in Les Verts v European Parliament [1986] held that:

options.A: The European Parliament had no legal personality and could not be sued
options.B: The Treaties constitute a 'constitutional charter of a Community based on the rule of law' in which acts of institutions are subject to review for conformity with the Treaties
options.C: Member states retained sovereign immunity from EU law claims
options.D: The Commission had no power to bring infringement proceedings against member states for breach of unwritten general principles

questions.explanation: In Les Verts (Case 294/83), the Court of Justice held that 'the EEC Treaty, albeit concluded in the form of an international agreement, nonetheless constitutes the constitutional charter of a Community based on the rule of law.' This was a foundational statement of the constitutional nature of the Treaties — the EU is a community of law, not just an international organisation.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a source of primary EU law?

options.B: The Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union
options.C: A Directive adopted unanimously by the Council on a proposal from the Commission

questions.explanation: Directives — even those adopted unanimously — are secondary law, adopted by EU institutions under powers conferred by the Treaties. They must comply with primary law. Primary law consists of the Treaties (TEU and TFEU), the Charter, Protocols/Annexes, and accession treaties. Secondary law (Regulations, Directives, Decisions) occupies a lower tier.

questions.question: Article 2 TEU, which sets out the values of the EU (human dignity, freedom, democracy, equality, rule of law, and human rights), is significant because:

options.A: It provides the legal basis for all EU legislation
options.B: It forms the foundational value statement of the Union, and breach of these values by a member state triggers the Article 7 TEU sanction procedure
options.C: It requires member states to incorporate these values into their national constitutions
options.D: It gives the Court of Justice jurisdiction to review national court decisions for compliance with EU values

questions.explanation: Article 2 TEU identifies the foundational values of the EU. Article 7 TEU provides a procedure for responding to a 'clear risk of a serious breach' or 'serious and persistent breach' of Article 2 values by a member state — potentially resulting in suspension of voting rights. This mechanism has been used against Poland and Hungary.

questions.question: Opinion 2/13 of the Court of Justice (2014) is significant because it:

options.A: Confirmed that the ECHR had been incorporated into EU primary law
options.B: Found that the draft agreement for EU accession to the ECHR was incompatible with the EU Treaties, blocking accession
options.C: Approved the proposed Constitutional Treaty as compatible with the existing Treaties
options.D: Held that the Charter of Fundamental Rights did not apply to member states implementing EU law

questions.explanation: In Opinion 2/13, the CJEU found that the draft accession agreement under which the EU would join the ECHR was incompatible with EU law on multiple grounds — including the autonomy of EU law, the exclusive jurisdiction of the CJEU, and Protocol 16 ECHR. This effectively blocked EU accession to the ECHR, leaving Article 6(2) TEU's accession requirement unimplemented.
